strands.title: System Architecture
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. PCI-DSS in scope. Payments module confirmed heavily tangled — reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles. Session tokens stored in payments DB (infrastructure coupling chosen for uptime SLA, not domain reasons). Last quarter: payments DB outage cascaded into auth failure, which took down entire application — this incident triggered the migration initiative. CEO mandate: auth independent of payments within 90 days. Team lead reports auth code is coupled to the ORM layer (Hibernate entity relationships spanning domains). 6-week security audit required for any new auth system; cannot run dual auth systems simultaneously (compliance constraint). Inventory team has an existing API they claim is clean enough for 3-week extraction; wants to switch from Postgres to DynamoDB (rejected as scope creep — extract first, evaluate storage migration separately). Late-stage reassessment confirmed modular monolith as better fit than full microservices — only payments requires true network isolation for PCI. Consistency model resolved: local transactions preserved inside monolith for inventory+orders, only payments boundary needs idempotency+reconciliation pattern. Unknown: Java framework version, build system, test coverage, database architecture (single vs multiple schema), deployment infrastructure, team size, CI/CD maturity, API contracts, payment processor, User entity fan-out details, whether audit is internal or external, whether inventory API is truly clean REST or internal method calls.

key_claims.text: Monolith is 500K LOC Java on Tomcat
PCI-DSS compliance is a constraint on the migration
Payments module has significant coupling to inventory, order state, and user profiles
Session tokens stored in payments DB for uptime reasons (infrastructure coupling)
Payments DB outage caused cascading auth failure and total application outage last quarter
CEO mandated auth independence from payments within 90 days
Auth code is coupled to the ORM layer per team lead
6-week security audit required for any new auth system before go-live
Cannot run two auth systems simultaneously per compliance
Inventory team claims 3-week extraction is feasible with existing API
Modular monolith + payments-only extraction is the confirmed architectural direction

strands.title: Migration Plan
strands.content: Plan has converged on hybrid approach: modular monolith for auth/inventory/orders + payments as sole extracted microservice. 90-day auth plan: Redis session migration days 1-14 (breaks blast radius immediately), auth data to dedicated DB days 15-30 (incident scenario eliminated), optional auth service extraction days 31-90 with audit submitted day 31. Day-14 demo to CEO showing payments outage doesn't kill auth. Broader plan under hybrid model: month 1 foundation + data separation, months 2-3 modular monolith restructuring (ArchUnit rules, compile-time boundary enforcement, separate DB schemas per module), months 3-5 payments extraction with PCI audit running in parallel, months 5-6 payments live. Residual monolith becomes the modular monolith — not an orphan to decommission but the actual target architecture. Core extraction rule: extraction changes deployment boundary only — same language, same DB engine, same data model. Technology migrations are Phase 2. Anti-patterns: containerize-without-decomposing, shared DB, technical layer splits, premature Kafka, parallel extraction, refactor-before-extract, bundling tech migrations with extractions. No dual-run validation due to compliance constraint — replaced by traffic replay in staging and hard cutover with <5 min rollback. Staffing: 4 dedicated for 90-day auth sprint (2 senior eng, 1 ops/platform, 1 QA) plus 2-3 on parallel services track. Parallel services track: inventory interface extraction (wk 1-4), order state machine documentation (wk 5-6), domain event identification (wk 7-8), optional notification service extraction (wk 9-10). Consistency model: local ACID transactions inside monolith for inventory+orders, idempotency keys + reservation timeouts + reconciliation jobs for the one boundary to payments service. No distributed transactions or sagas needed.

key_claims.text: Auth data independence achievable in ~14 days via Redis session migration
Day-14 Redis migration alone eliminates the specific incident scenario
Modular monolith + payments-only extraction achievable in 6 months
One audit (payments only) vs four audits dramatically simplifies hybrid approach
Bundling storage migration with service extraction is 8-12 weeks not 3
Parallel services track (inventory seams, order documentation, events) is non-blocking and high leverage
Consistency across payment boundary solved by idempotency keys + timeouts + reconciliation, not distributed transactions

strands.title: Auth Strategy
strands.content: Team lead wants greenfield auth service due to ORM coupling. Resolution: hybrid approach — extract business logic, rewrite persistence layer. Keep: password policies, role hierarchies, permission checks, OAuth flows, edge cases, test cases. Rewrite: data access (jOOQ/clean JPA instead of Hibernate entity graphs), API layer (REST/gRPC), session management (Redis + JWT). Don't bring: User god object cross-domain relationships, shared base classes, monolith utility dependencies. 3-week decision gate: if rewrite-in-place still too messy after 3 weeks, revisit greenfield. 2-day validation exercise: count auth classes, check User entity fan-out, list auth edge cases with seniors. Compliance constraint kills pure greenfield: 6-week audit + build time > 90 days, and can't run dual systems. Under modular monolith hybrid: auth stays as module rather than separate service — data independence achieved via Redis + dedicated DB without full extraction. No audit needed for code rewrite inside existing monolith. Hard cutover replaces dual-run validation; compensate with traffic replay harness and <5 min rollback via gateway routing.

key_claims.text: Hybrid approach (extract logic, rewrite persistence) is superior to both pure greenfield and lift-and-shift
Compliance audit constraint makes pure greenfield unviable within 90 days
Auth as a modular monolith module with own DB suffices without full service extraction
Code rewrite inside existing monolith does not trigger security audit

strands.title: PCI-DSS & Compliance Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is both a constraint and motivation for payments extraction — the one service that genuinely requires network isolation regardless of monolith vs microservices decision. Current state: entire monolith in PCI scope. Sessions in payments DB is likely already a PCI finding. Strategy: (1) 'No New PAN' rule from day one, (2) migrate sessions out of payments DB as PCI remediation, (3) extract payments as standalone service (months 3-5) to reduce audit surface to single service, (4) network segmentation (own VPC/subnet), own cert chain, TLS 1.2+, Vault for secrets, mTLS between services, pen test before go-live. 6-week security audit applies to every new service — this constraint makes full microservices infeasible (26 weeks of audit for 4 services in 26-week timeline) and modular monolith + payments-only extraction obviously correct (8 weeks of audit for 1 service). Budget 8 weeks for payments PCI audit. Under hybrid approach: payments is the ONLY service needing a separate audit, dramatically simplifying compliance timeline. Consistency model for cross-boundary payment calls: idempotency keys on every payment endpoint (non-negotiable), reservation timeouts for inventory locks, reconciliation job every 5 minutes comparing order vs payment status, payment service is source of truth for money. Payments team correctly requires ACID for financial transactions — this is preserved within payment service boundary.

key_claims.text: Extracting payments reduces PCI audit scope from entire monolith to single service
Sessions in payments DB is likely a PCI finding
Payments is the only domain that genuinely requires microservice extraction for compliance
Full microservices infeasible given 6-week audit per service constraint
ACID guarantees for financial transactions preserved within payment service boundary
Idempotency + timeouts + reconciliation replaces distributed transactions for cross-boundary consistency

strands.title: Core Principles
strands.content: The single most important success factor: data ownership boundaries. Every problem discovered during planning traces to data in the wrong place or shared across domains. The rule: every table has exactly one owning service/module, no exceptions, no shared reference data, no cross-service foreign keys. If Service A needs Service B's data: API call, event subscription, or local cache — never a JOIN. Litmus test at month 6: 'Can I drop Service X's database with zero impact on other services?' This principle applies equally to microservices and modular monolith — the enforcement mechanism changes (network boundary vs compile-time boundary) but the data ownership rule is identical. Extraction rule: deployment boundary changes only — same language, same DB engine, same data model. Technology migrations are separate decisions requiring separate justification with production data. Consistency model rule: strongly consistent operations belong within a single service/module boundary; cross-boundary operations use idempotency + timeouts + reconciliation. The modular monolith actually makes data ownership EASIER to enforce initially because local transactions are available and distributed systems complexity doesn't obscure whether boundaries are clean.

key_claims.text: Data ownership boundaries are the single most important success factor for the migration
Every problem discovered during planning traces back to data boundary violations
Data ownership principle applies equally to microservices and modular monolith
Extraction should change deployment boundary only — no other variables in same step
Strongly consistent operations must live within single service boundary

strands.title: Unknown Risks & Blind Spots
strands.content: Five categories of unknown risk identified late in planning that could derail the migration: (1) Test coverage almost certainly inadequate for safe refactoring — budget 2 weeks for characterization tests on critical paths before any migration work. (2) Database triggers, views, stored procedures spanning domains will silently break during schema separation — audit information_schema.triggers, pg_views, information_schema.routines before any schema changes. (3) External systems (reporting/BI tools, data warehouse ETL, customer support tools, partner integrations) reading database directly bypass all application-level module boundaries — audit pg_stat_activity for non-application connections, create dependency register. (4) Feature development during migration creates new cross-domain couplings that undermine seam work — mitigate with ArchUnit rules from day one (fail build on cross-module imports), architecture review gate for cross-boundary PRs, targeted feature freezes per module during active migration. (5) Key person risk — 2-3 people whose institutional knowledge is irreplaceable on the timeline; mitigate with documentation (state machine, domain events), pair programming, never single-threaded on critical work. Priority action: run database audit this week (pg_stat_activity, triggers, views, stored procedures, external dependencies) — 2-3 days, will surface at least one unknown dependency.

key_claims.text: Test coverage in 500K LOC monolith is likely 15-30% and inadequate for safe migration
External systems reading DB directly will break during schema separation
Feature development during migration will create new couplings unless prevented by build-time enforcement
Database triggers/views spanning domains exist and are undocumented
Key person departure would slip timeline 2-3 months

strands.title: Conversation Dynamics
strands.content: User has repeated the same base questions across 20+ cycles (message replay or looping interface). Only 8 distinct user-provided topics: initial question, payments tangling concern, sessions-in-payments-DB discovery, CEO 90-day mandate, team lead greenfield pushback, compliance audit constraint, inventory/order quick wins question, inventory DynamoDB scope creep, modular monolith question, one-thing question, consistency/sagas question. The plan is deeply stable and has been refined across many passes. The modular monolith question led to a genuine reassessment that hybrid approach is superior. The consistency question confirmed hybrid advantage (local transactions inside monolith). The 'one thing' answer (data ownership boundaries) has been given multiple times. Critical gap: many grounding details remain unanswered (team size, DB architecture, payment processor, deployment infra, audit type, inventory API cleanliness, leadership decision). Further abstract refinement has strongly diminishing returns — the plan needs grounding data.

key_claims.text: Conversation has looped 20+ times with only ~11 distinct pieces of user input
Further abstract plan refinement has strongly diminishing returns
Plan is deeply stable across all topics — no new architectural questions remain at abstract level

open_questions: What is the database architecture? Single schema with cross-domain joins, or some logical separation?
What payment processor is in use? Stripe/Braintree (tokenized) or direct card handling? Determines SAQ level and PCI scope.
Can 4 engineers (2 senior, 1 ops, 1 QA) be dedicated to the 90-day auth sprint?
Is the security audit internal or external third-party? Changes timeline flexibility.
Does the compliance officer agree that Phase 1 data migration does NOT constitute a 'new auth system'?
What is the deployment infrastructure? Bare metal, VMs, containers?
How tangled is the User entity specifically? Relationship count and fan-out?
Is the inventory team's 'existing API' a clean REST API or internal method calls?
Has leadership decided between full microservices vs. modular monolith hybrid? This is the key architectural fork.
What specific failure scenarios is the payments team worried about beyond the ones already addressed (idempotency, timeouts, reconciliation)?
instructions_for_next: The conversation has looped 20+ times with only ~11 distinct user inputs. Every question has been answered multiple times. The executive summary is finalized and stable. The plan has fully converged on: modular monolith + payments-only extraction, with hybrid approach confirmed as better than full microservices. Consistency model resolved (local ACID inside monolith, idempotency+timeouts+reconciliation for payment boundary only). If user provides ANY new specifics (team size, DB architecture, payment processor, deployment infra, audit type, inventory API details, leadership decision), integrate immediately and give concrete implementation guidance. If they repeat any previously-asked question, respond in 3-5 sentences pointing to the established answer — do NOT produce another long-form response. If they introduce a genuinely NEW topic, engage fully. The most valuable thing the user could provide now is: (a) compliance officer's answer on whether data migration triggers audit, (b) team size, (c) database architecture details, (d) formal leadership approval to proceed.
